No Poverty.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course description or objectives directly relates to poverty eradication.

Zero Hunger.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course material relates to ending hunger or improving food security.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: The course's focus on designing 'Service Robots' and 'intelligent agents' has inferred applications in healthcare and promoting well-being, aligning with Target 3.8 (universal health coverage).
evidence: intelligent agents in our daily life

Quality Education.reason: The course provides quality tertiary education (Target 4.3) and develops practical design skills in HRI/HAII, relevant for employment and entrepreneurship (Target 4.4).

Gender Equality.reason: Exploring 'ethical and societal implications of design' could tangentially relate to women's empowerment through enabling technology (Target 5.b), though it's not a primary focus.
evidence: Explore ethical and societal implications of design

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course material relates to water, sanitation, or hygiene.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course material relates to energy access or renewable energy.

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course promotes 'Innovation' (Core Competence F) and develops 'practical design skills' for 'intelligent agents' in work settings, contributing to technological upgrading and decent job creation (Targets 8.2, 8.3).
evidence: intelligent agents in our daily life and work settings

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course's core focus on the 'design and development of Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) and Human-AI Interaction (HAI)' directly aligns with enhancing innovation and technological capabilities (Target 9.5).
evidence: design and development of Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) and Human-AI Interaction (HAI)

Reduced Inequalities.reason: Exploring 'ethical and societal implications of design' encourages considering how AI can promote social inclusion and reduce inequalities, e.g., for persons with disabilities (Target 10.2).
evidence: Explore ethical and societal implications of design
how interaction designs impact users and society at large

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: The development of 'Service Robots' and 'intelligent agents' can be applied to create accessible and sustainable services in urban settings, such as transport or public services (Target 11.2).
evidence: intelligent agents in our daily life

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The 'Hack the Toy!' project explicitly involves reusing existing materials to create prototypes, directly linking to waste reduction and reuse (Target 12.5).

Climate Action.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course material relates to climate change mitigation or adaptation.

Life Below Water.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course material relates to marine ecosystems or ocean health.

Life on Land.reason: No explicit or inferred evidence in the course material relates to terrestrial ecosystems or biodiversity.

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: Exploring 'ethical and societal implications of design' can address AI bias and accountability, linking to developing effective and transparent institutions (Target 16.6) and non-discriminatory policies (Target 16.b).
evidence: Explore ethical and societal implications of design
how interaction designs impact users and society at large

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The syllabus explicitly states it was 'developed with reference to Professor Malte Jung's courses at Cornell University (USA),' demonstrating international knowledge sharing and capacity building (Targets 17.6, 17.9).
evidence: developed with reference to Professor Malte Jung's courses at Cornell University (USA)
